albums.title: Discovery
albums.reason: With Digital Love, One More Time, and Superheroes all in your all-time top tracks, this is the album you clearly know best — but when did you last sit with it front to back?

albums.title: In Rainbows
albums.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track of all time — In Rainbows is its home, and it holds up as one of their most emotionally direct records.

albums.title: The Dark Side of the Moon
albums.reason: With nearly 1,200 all-time plays, Pink Floyd is deep in your vault — this is the one to revisit when you want something that demands full attention.

albums.title: Moon Safari
albums.reason: Air sits at over 1,000 all-time plays and Moon Safari is their pristine peak — hazy, warm, and impossible to rush.

albums.title: Melody A.M.
albums.reason: Over 1,000 plays puts Röyksopp firmly in your bedrock, and this debut is the one that defined their sound — downtempo Scandinavian bliss at its finest.

albums.title: Leftism
albums.reason: Release the Pressure is your fourth most-played track ever, and Leftism is the album it lives on — a landmark of UK electronic music worth returning to.

albums.title: Is This It
albums.reason: With 652 all-time plays, The Strokes are a long-standing love — this debut is their tightest, most essential statement and deserves another spin.

albums.title: Orbital 2 (The Brown Album)
albums.reason: Orbital clocks 777 all-time plays in your library — this second album is their rawest and most hypnotic, perfect for deep listening.

albums.title: Galactic Melt
albums.reason: Your love of Daft Punk and Justice points straight here — Com Truise builds lush synthwave with the same cinematic sweep but a more melancholic, lo-fi texture.

albums.title: II
albums.reason: If Radiohead's electronic edge and James Blake's intimacy both resonate with you, Moderat sits precisely in that gap — dense, emotional, and masterfully produced.

albums.title: Music Has the Right to Children
albums.reason: Your deep runs with Röyksopp and Air suggest you love downtempo with emotional depth — Boards of Canada perfected that hazy, childhood-memory quality on this record.

albums.title: Suzuki
albums.reason: With Röyksopp and Air as all-time staples, Tosca's blend of Viennese jazz-electronica and warm trip-hop is a natural next step you've probably overlooked.

albums.title: Immunity
albums.reason: Your Leftfield and Underworld plays show you love UK electronic music with weight and craft — Immunity moves from crushing club tracks to heartbreaking ambient, often in the same breath.

albums.title: Dive
albums.reason: Given your love of Air and Röyksopp, Tycho's warm, guitar-laced electronic instrumentals should feel instantly familiar — Dive is the album where he hit his stride.

albums.title: The Last Resort
albums.reason: Your affection for Leftfield and Underworld suggests you enjoy electronic music with atmosphere and tension — Trentemøller's debut album is a cult classic in exactly that vein.

albums.title: Since I Left You
albums.reason: Daft Punk's sample-based genius is among your all-time loves — The Avalanches built this album from thousands of samples into something equally ecstatic and completely unique.

albums.title: Elaenia
albums.reason: Your recent deep dives into Four Tet, Weval, and DJ Koze suggest a love of electronic music that breathes — Floating Points weaves jazz chords and organic texture into something that feels alive.

albums.title: Con Todo El Mundo
albums.reason: Your heavy plays of Bill Withers and Charles Bradley show a love of soulful, feel-good warmth — Khruangbin draws on Thai funk, soul, and psych in a way that feels effortlessly cool.

albums.title: Knee-Deep in the North Sea
albums.reason: You've been spending serious time with Hania Rani and Nils Frahm — Portico Quartet lives in a similar contemplative space but routes it through jazz instrumentation and hang drum.

albums.title: Persona
albums.reason: Your recent plays of Max Cooper and Dominik Eulberg point toward electronic music with structure and feeling — Rival Consoles makes modular-inflected music with the same careful emotional intent.

albums.title: Black Focus
albums.reason: With Oscar Peterson Trio and Bill Withers dominating your recent listening, you're clearly in a groove-and-soul headspace — Yussef Kamaal's debut fuses jazz fluency with raw dancefloor energy.

albums.title: Spells
albums.reason: Your devotion to Nils Frahm and Hania Rani suggests a taste for precise, emotional piano-led composition — Boysen works in a similar Berlin contemporary-classical space with darker, more dramatic results.

albums.title: We Like It Here
albums.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio runs and soul explorations via Bill Withers and Charles Bradley suggest an appetite for virtuosic, feel-good ensemble playing — Snarky Puppy are the modern heirs to that spirit.

albums.title: Erased Tapes Collection I
albums.reason: Your consistent listening to Brambles, Ólafur Arnalds, and Nils Frahm puts you right in the heart of the Erased Tapes sound — this label compilation is the perfect door into its wider world.

albums.title: Prehension
albums.reason: Joep Beving is your most-played artist this week by a wide margin — Prehension is his debut and the most stripped-back expression of his quiet, searching style.

albums.title: Waltz for Debby
albums.reason: You've been deep in Bill Evans this month — Waltz for Debby is the classic you haven't had on your recent list, recorded live at the Village Vanguard with extraordinary warmth.

albums.title: Geography
albums.reason: Tom Misch is in your recent rotation and Geography is the album that made his name — a sun-soaked blend of jazz guitar, neo-soul, and bedroom production.

albums.title: Smokers Delight
albums.reason: Nightmares on Wax has been part of your month and this 1995 landmark is their warmest and most transportive album — a record that sounds incredible late at night.

albums.title: Little Ghost
albums.reason: Moonchild is in your recent listening and this debut is where their soft, flute-led jazz-soul signature first crystallised — intimate, unhurried, and beautifully played.

albums.title: We Get Requests
albums.reason: You've been playing Oscar Peterson obsessively this month — We Get Requests is one of his most joyful and melodic records, lighter in touch than Night Train and worth exploring next.

albums.title: Solo
albums.reason: Nils Frahm features across your recent and weekly listening — Solo is his most unguarded record, recorded alone on a grand piano in the early hours, utterly unhurried.

albums.title: The Ballad of Raven and the Tide
albums.reason: Folamour appeared in your recent plays and this album showcases his most soulful, jazz-inflected house — rich with live instrumentation and emotional weight.

songs.title: Weird Fishes / Arpeggi
songs.reason: Reckoner is your third most-played track ever, and this is its companion piece on In Rainbows — one of the most cathartic builds in the Radiohead catalogue.

songs.title: Something About Us
songs.reason: You've played six Daft Punk tracks extensively in your all-time top 20, but this quietly devastating Discovery ballad deserves its own moment of focus.

songs.title: The Wilhelm Scream
songs.reason: Postpone is your most-played track ever and CMYK is in your top 20 — The Wilhelm Scream is the James Blake song that tends to hit hardest on a revisit.

songs.title: Cowgirl
songs.reason: Born Slippy is deep in your all-time plays — Cowgirl from Dubnobasswithmyheadman is the track that surrounds it on their best album and builds just as relentlessly.

songs.title: La Femme d'Argent
songs.reason: Over 1,000 plays with Air and this opener from Moon Safari is one of the most hypnotic pieces they ever recorded — seven minutes that feel both endless and too short.

songs.title: When the Levee Breaks
songs.reason: Nearly 1,000 all-time plays with Led Zeppelin — this closing track from Led Zeppelin IV is one of the heaviest things they ever committed to tape.

songs.title: Poor Leno
songs.reason: Over 1,000 all-time plays puts Röyksopp firmly in your core — Poor Leno is the Melody A.M. track that tends to linger longest after you hear it.

songs.title: D.A.N.C.E.
songs.reason: Justice sits at 771 all-time plays and this is their most joyful moment — a perfect pop-electronic hybrid that sounds just as alive today.

songs.title: Flightwave
songs.reason: Your deep love of Daft Punk's more cinematic electronic moments — Veridis Quo, Superheroes — makes this Com Truise highlight a natural fit.

songs.title: Open Eye Signal
songs.reason: Your Leftfield and Orbital all-time plays show you love electronic music that builds with intention — Open Eye Signal is one of the decade's great peak-time tracks.

songs.title: Roygbiv
songs.reason: Your Air and Röyksopp affection points to a love of melody-first electronica — this Boards of Canada gem is two minutes of pure, aching warmth.

songs.title: Moan
songs.reason: Your Leftfield plays — especially Release the Pressure — show you love deep, slow-burn electronic tension, and this is Trentemøller at his most mesmerising.

songs.title: Bad Kingdom
songs.reason: The emotional weight of your James Blake and Radiohead plays suggests this will land hard — Bad Kingdom is Moderat's most direct, devastating track.

songs.title: Awake
songs.reason: Given how much Air and Röyksopp feature in your all-time listening, this warm, guitar-driven instrumental should feel immediately right.

songs.title: Honey
songs.reason: Your sustained love of Air and Röyksopp — both artists who blend jazz sensibility with electronics — makes this Tosca highlight an easy recommendation.

songs.title: Frontier Psychiatrist
songs.reason: Daft Punk's mastery of sampling is one of your all-time loves — this Avalanches single takes that art form somewhere entirely stranger and more exhilarating.

songs.title: LesAlpx
songs.reason: Your recent Four Tet and Weval plays point toward electronic music with jazz texture — this Floating Points standout from Elaenia is one of the most beautiful things in that space.

songs.title: Maria También
songs.reason: Your Bill Withers soul deep-dive and warm groove sensibility this year makes this effortlessly cool Khruangbin track a natural discovery.

songs.title: Strings of Light
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio obsession and soul listening via Withers and Bradley show a hunger for feel-good musicianship — this Yussef Kamaal track channels exactly that energy.

songs.title: Unfolding
songs.reason: Your recent Max Cooper and Dominik Eulberg plays suggest a love of electronic music with architecture — Rival Consoles builds with the same patient, purposeful approach.

songs.title: Ruins
songs.reason: Your consistent time with Hania Rani and Brambles shows a deep love of quiet, spacious music — Portico Quartet's Ruins has the same meditative quality routed through jazz.

songs.title: Gravity
songs.reason: You've been deep in Nils Frahm and Hania Rani — Boysen sits in that same Berlin-adjacent contemporary classical world but with a darker, more cinematic pull.

songs.title: Lingus
songs.reason: Your Oscar Peterson Trio devotion shows you love virtuosic ensemble jazz — Lingus is the Snarky Puppy live performance that tends to convert anyone who hears it.

songs.title: Khmer
songs.reason: Your recent blend of jazz exploration and ambient electronics — via Apparat, Ólafur Arnalds, and Oscar Peterson — makes this atmospheric Norwegian trumpeter a compelling next step.

songs.title: Sleeping Lotus
songs.reason: You've been playing Joep Beving more than anyone else this week — this piece from Prehension captures his most hushed and searching quality.

songs.title: Peace Piece
songs.reason: Bill Evans has been a strong presence in your month — this solo improvisation over a single sustained note is one of the most hypnotic things in the jazz piano canon.

songs.title: Hymn to Freedom
songs.reason: You've played Oscar Peterson Trio more than anyone in the last month — this soaring, gospel-inflected piece is one of his most emotionally direct performances.

songs.title: Movie
songs.reason: Tom Misch is in your recent rotation and Movie — from Geography — is the track that best captures his effortless feel-good guitar work and production warmth.

songs.title: Says
songs.reason: Nils Frahm features across both your weekly and monthly listening — Says is his most hypnotic long-form piece, building imperceptibly from almost nothing.

songs.title: The List
songs.reason: Moonchild appeared in your recent month's listening and this track from Little Ghost showcases their lightest, most intimate side — flute and vocals barely above a whisper.

songs.title: JSM
songs.reason: scruscru has been a clear fixture in your week — JSM is the title track of the EP you've been returning to, and it anchors their warm, live-instrument club sound.

songs.title: Our Own Eternity (Our Own Reality)
songs.reason: Folamour appeared in your recent plays and this track is his most celebrated moment — a deeply moving, gospel-inflected house record that fits the reflective mood of your current listening perfectly.

djSets.setName: HMWL Podcast 430: Niconé & David Hasert (Kompakt / Get Physical / HMWL)
Daphni - FOLD, London. Nov 22, 2025. 7hr DJ set
Klausmarie b2b Alec Troniq - RAVIN' RAVIN' 2026 @ Sektor Evolution
BENT THE KNEE TO HER VOL. 1
